text: she pressed the cloth of her headwrap to her forehead, soaking up some of the sweat. She picked up a rectangular object to move it aside. A shard of glass fell from its bottom, clanking on a piece of concrete . Lara turned the item over. The front had a pane of cracked glass set into it, and there was something beneath. She shook the shards onto the ground and examined the photo . There were two people in it, a man a woman. He was dressed all in black, save for a bit of white showing beneath his coat and the blue cloth tied at his neck. He gazed at the woman tenderly. And she…she was stunning. Her blonde hair hung in ringlets down her back, shining like gold, and her pale skin was clean and unblemished. She wore a crisp, white gown, decorated with delicate patterns and little white beads. The woman’s smile was radiant, and her eyes sparkled . Lara wasn’t sure how long she stared at the picture; it was a timeless moment, and everything else fell away around her. The beauty and joy captured in the photo left her speechless . And it had been buried, forgotten, for countless years . A drop of water pelted the photo. Lara’s brows fell in confusion. She raised a hand to her cheek, only to find it dry. Another drop landed on the beautiful woman’s face. Lara looked up; the black clouds were overhead now, and a strong wind blew through the ruins . “Damn,” Lara rasped. She stuffed the framed photo into her bag to keep it safe. It had no value, but she couldn’t just leave it out here to be destroyed. The harshness of the world had already taken the people in the picture, but Lara could at least protect the last memory of them . She hurried her search, discovering a couple of crushed cans — a small victory. The sporadic rain suddenly became a downpour, falling sideways in the strong wind. Blinking moisture from her eyes, she slung her bag over her shoulder. She’d have to hurry back to town. After a long dry spell, the first rain was always the worst, and the roads wouldn’t be able to handle the excess water . As she carefully made her way through the debris and back toward the path, a metal sheen caught her eye. Lara paused, narrowing her eyes and turning to find the source. She spotted it in a heap of rubble. Before she lost sight of it again, she climbed onto the pile, testing her footing on the loose pieces, and reached into the gap. The tips of her fingers touched the object. She raked them over it, dragging it close enough to grab . She didn’t stop to examine it until she was on solid ground. It was a small box, fitting neatly in her palm. One side was discolored and falling apart, but the metal band around its middle was surprisingly clean. Turning it, she found the hinge along the back side and opened it . Lara released a long exhalation, and her lungs refused to fill for several seconds. When she caught her breath, she laughed, bouncing on her feet . “ Holy shit .” Nestled within in a patch of dark fabric, untouched by the sun and weather, was a ring. The band was gold, with a clear stone inlaid atop it. It was precious metal. A tiny, seemingly insignificant trinket that could nonetheless keep her well-fed for weeks . Tentatively, she ran a finger down one of its smooth sides . “It’s real. It’s really real .” The rain pelted her, stinging even through her clothes, but Lara grinned. She needed to get home. For the first time in a long while, things were going well. She’d nearly forgotten what hope felt like . She walked toward the road, swinging her bag around to her front. Her foot caught on some debris before she could stow the ring. She fell, feeling something tear in her boot as she landed in the rushing water along the side of the road . Lara’s heart stopped. She watched the box fall from her hand, watched the ring tumble out of it, watched the water current sweep the bit of gold toward a cluttered storm drain . “No!” She scrambled on hands and knees, sputtering water splashed into her face, ignoring the pain in her palms and knees as debris cut into her skin. But she wasn’t fast enough. The ring disappeared into the trash and grime gathered around the drain . “No!” she screamed again, leaping at the gutter. She dug through the mud, tossing aside jagged chunks of concrete, tufts of dead grass, and scraps of wood and cloth . “No, no, no. You can’t do this to me!” She pounded her fists into the flowing water, hitting the rough ground beneath it. It would be impossible to find the ring in the foamy, murky runoff, amidst all the rubble . “You just can’t.” Her voice broke, and she hung her head . * * * R onin watched the redhaired woman from behind a mess of rotting lumber, bricks, and sun-faded shingles that had once been a building. The raging wind and heavy rain made it impossible for his audio receptors to distinguish her words, but he detected desperation and despair in her tone. For all that, she appeared physically unharmed . Why had she come out here alone? This far from the settlement, she was exposed and vulnerable. The perfect prey for wandering reavers or other scavengers. Ronin had seen humans and bots alike end one another to seize any advantage, however slight, that would aid in survival . The woman, propped on hands and knees in the gutter, bowed her head. Her shoulders shook, and the soaked cloths wrapped around her hung into the steadily rising water. He had no reason to go to her.
